speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Mr. Kurt Saunders-- August the 16th, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this is a family picture taken on my daughter Gayle's grounds in High Falls again. The guy with his mouth wide open, naturally that's me. Sitting on my lap is Shayna. That's my son's daughter. The next here, the little boy smiling, that's my daughter Gayle's twin. His name is Max. Behind him is his cousin, Ryan. His mouth is open too. After all, he's my grandson. And behind me is his-- is Max's brother Ethan, who is my daughter's twins-- the other twin. In front of my wife-- my wife is holding on to the chair-- sits Jared. I think when the picture was taken, he was about a year old. So that's four years ago, roughly. And that's the family picture. You got the whole gang there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. --and yours. We'd like you to introduce your family, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Here's my daughter Karen; daughter Gayle; my two grandsons behind me, Ethan and Max. We've spoken about them before, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the family--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that's here today.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's here today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have a son too, and he has three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. I'd like to ask both of you daughters, how much did you know of your father's story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything, since the time I can remember. Yeah, I-- I grew up with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think the same thing, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: He always talked about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was it presented to you as?

speaker: interviewee
text: As something that should never be forgotten.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was it a natural presentation, or were-- were you sort of sat down and preached to? Or did he--

speaker: interviewee
text: Naturally. He told us stories. It was stories and stories--

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a storyteller.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and stories, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: He just always told stories.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell us about the relatives, tell about escape, tell them about happen. I remember you telling me about the grandfather rubbing lye on-- on the ground and him rubbing, cleaning the streets. You know, the battering of children against trees as a sport. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never heard that one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's older. She's older. [LAUGHTER] How did his story impact on your lives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, profoundly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the way you were raised.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would say on the-- on the-- on the positive side, there were-- there was my father's example and stories taught us from a-- a very early age. We could tell that it was important that we knew how to take care of ourselves, knew how to be self-sufficient, knew how to use our heads-- just an independence of spirit. Also by example, a always looking to the future. Even though he tells stories about the past, he didn't live in the past. He always looked ahead so that was a very strong, strong example.

speaker: interviewee
text: Living life to its fullest. We were not allowed to sleep late on Saturdays, because you would be wasting time. And even though he worked seven days a week, there were always-- we-- as kids, we were always traveling on Sundays-- going some place, doing something, seeing something. You know, living life-- because you don't know what tomorrow will bring.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your Jewish lives? Were you identifying with any Jewish institutions or Jewish movement or anything of that sort? Jewish rituals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well we were-- we were brought up in the Reform Judaism and went to temple [INAUDIBLE] holidays. But it wasn't so much a religion as a people that we belonged to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and feeling that-- Well, I did too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but all the way through high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about you guys? Did you know about your grandfather's story? ETHAN FELDMAN Well, I knew a little bit of it. But-- What did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew how-- sort of how I escaped. Uh, sort of how I escaped. But--

speaker: interviewee
text: How he escaped.

speaker: interviewee
text: How he-- how he escaped. I didn't really get all the details. I was young when he told me. I was younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, maybe you'll have a lot of the details now from this tape. How about you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know because he told me about him-- it-- because-- because just last year, he went to our school-- my school in the city-- and told how he escaped.

speaker: interviewee
text: Talked to his class.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talked to you, mhmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it wasn't profound, you know, as far as I go. I know. He was different than all the other fathers of the kids I hung around with. You know, he had gone through something that I had thought as a child didn't really happen, until I met relatives that sort of popped up as the years went by. And then I went, oh my god, these stories are really true. You know, only because of the fact we lived in America, you know? And it was sort of, we lived in the land of plenty, and this didn't happen. And how could this have happened? Because he's here, you know? I mean, I didn't even understand that he had an accent, you know. To me, my father spoke like everybody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all the other kids would say, your father speaks with an accent. And we'd go, no, he doesn't.

words.text: your father speaks with an accent.
And we'd go, no, he doesn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they would say to me, oh, yeah. It's so great. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you two boys go to Hebrew school, or do you get lessons? Have you learned anything about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Our bar mitzvah is next year, and I learned-- I don't learn much about the Holocaust with my teacher. She-- she comes to our house in New York City, every week. And I think this year she's coming twice a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. You too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You too. And you are learning things about Jewish life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we don't really learn about the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never-- I don't think I ever studied that with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't think-- she doesn't speak much about the Holocaust. She's mainly preparing them for the bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Her name is [? Rifka. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Do you want to add anything, Mr Saunders, to your testimony--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --today?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- I mentioned it to you before-- my daughters knew how I felt about intermarriages. And I was fortunate enough that they wound up with Jewish husbands, and my son wound up with a Jewish girl as a wife. And in one way I succeeded. I always felt that the only way I could really feel that I won, that I defeated Hitler, is if I can raise a family like this. I can raise two daughters and a son and give them an education. And talent, they had of their own. I couldn't make her a singer because she has it on my own. I couldn't hold a tune. So she didn't get it from me, but she got it from her mother and her own too. And the same with my daughter, Gayle. These girls have been successful because they wanted to be successful. And they had an example. I have to give more credit-- as much as seems that I get all the credit for this, I have to give credit for their mother. She really did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember these two stories-- to show you what the difference is growing up in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Remember why you told me why I should go to college?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to him, why is it so important? I don't understand this. Why should I be going to college? And he said to me, because I saw Jewish girls selling their body because they were not-- for stockings, he said-- because they were uneducated. He said, I feel that my children should be educated so in a time of trouble, if it ever happens again, that you would know what to do. You wouldn't have to sell your bodies on the street for a pair of stockings or whatever. You'd be able to have the education to maybe do something, take care of yourself and survive. And I'm just trying to think of one other thing-- going to temple. Because when you're like 13, 14, you're sometimes-- or 15-- you start to rebel. And you don't want to go on the, you know, on the holidays. And you don't want to go on Friday nights. And I remember him saying, if you're going to stay home from school, you have to go. And you have to go because you have to show the world that we are here, that we celebrate that we are Jews and that-- that we are visible to the Gentile world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other anecdotes that you wish [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't think of one at the moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank all of you for coming-- and especially you, Mr. Saunders, for giving the testimony to the Shoah Foundation. And we wish you all well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. You too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Thank you.
